subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Main Board & Maps
- **1** Association board (central shared board for partner zoos, universities, and conservation projects)
- **4** player zoo maps (double-sided A/B — side A for beginners, side B for advanced asymmetric layouts)
- **1** break token track (printed on Association board)

#### Cards
- **255** zoo cards total, divided into three types:
  - **Animal cards** — Each shows species name, continent icon(s), animal type icon(s), size requirement, cost, and appeal value
  - **Sponsor cards** — Ongoing effects or one-time bonuses that enhance your zoo engine
  - **Conservation project cards** — Endgame and in-game conservation point sources requiring specific animal collections
- **12** final scoring cards (2 dealt per slot, players draft 1 during setup)
- **8** base conservation project cards (always available on the Association board)
- **5** action cards per player (Cards, Build, Animals, Association, Sponsors — each with a side I and upgraded side II)

#### Tokens & Markers
- **Money tokens** — In denominations of 1, 2, 5, and 10
- **X-tokens** — Multi-use wild bonus tokens that can substitute for money, card draw, or other effects
- **Appeal markers** — 1 per player, tracked on the scoring track
- **Conservation markers** — 1 per player, tracked on the scoring track (runs opposite direction to appeal)
- **Reputation markers** — 1 per player, tracked on the reputation track on the Association board
- **Association workers** — Small meeples used for partner zoo and university actions
- **Multiplier tokens** — Used on the appeal/conservation track for values beyond the printed range

#### Association Board Components
- **Partner zoo tiles** — Placed on designated slots, each granting a unique bonus when visited by a worker
- **University tiles** — Placed on university slots, providing card draw or special abilities when visited
- **Conservation project slots** — Spaces for the 8 base conservation projects plus player-contributed projects

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Association Board (Central)
- **Place centrally** — Set the Association board in the middle of the table where all players can reach it.
- **Partner zoos** — Shuffle partner zoo tiles and place one on each designated partner zoo slot.
- **Universities** — Shuffle university tiles and place one on each university slot.
- **Base conservation projects** — Place all 8 base conservation project cards in the designated slots on the board.
- **Break track** — Place the break token on the starting position of the break track.
- **Reputation track** — Ensure the reputation track (0-15) is visible along the edge of the board.

#### Card Display
- **Zoo card deck** — Shuffle all 255 zoo cards thoroughly and place the deck face-down near the Association board.
- **Card display** — Reveal 6 cards from the top of the deck and lay them in a face-up row (positions 1 through 6, left to right).
- **Snap zone** — Position 6 (rightmost) is the snap zone; cards here can be snapped before being discarded.

#### Final Scoring Cards
- **Deal slots** — Place final scoring cards in pairs (2 per slot) near the board so players can draft during setup.

#### Player Zoo Maps
- **Side A** — Use for first games; all maps are identical with a balanced layout of building spaces.
- **Side B** — Use for experienced play; each map has a unique asymmetric layout with different terrain restrictions and bonus spaces.
- **Map features** — Each map shows a grid of buildable spaces, water areas (unbuildable), special bonus spaces, and slots for kiosks and pavilions.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Individual Player Components
- **Zoo map** — Each player takes 1 zoo map (all use side A for first game, or choose side B for variety).
- **Action cards** — Take your set of 5 action cards (Cards, Build, Animals, Association, Sponsors) with side I face-up.
- **Action card placement** — Place your 5 action cards in a row in slots numbered 1 through 5. The default starting arrangement is: Cards in slot 1, Build in slot 2, Animals in slot 3, Association in slot 4, Sponsors in slot 5.
- **Starting money** — Take 25 money from the supply.
- **Starting hand** — Draw 8 zoo cards from the deck. Keep all 8 as your starting hand.
- **Final scoring card** — Draw 2 final scoring cards, examine both, keep 1 and return the other to the box unseen.

#### Track Markers
- **Appeal marker** — Place at position 0 on the appeal track.
- **Conservation marker** — Place at position 0 on the conservation track.
- **Reputation marker** — Place at position 1 on the reputation track on the Association board.

#### Association Workers
- **Starting workers** — Each player begins with a limited supply of association workers (exact count depends on player count).
- **Worker reserve** — Additional workers can be unlocked through reputation advancement and certain sponsor/animal effects.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### Determining First Player
- **Random selection** — Determine the starting player randomly (e.g., the player who most recently visited a zoo).
- **Turn order** — Play proceeds clockwise from the starting player.

#### Initial Board State
- **Empty zoo** — All players begin with completely empty zoo maps (no enclosures, no animals, no buildings).
- **Full hand** — Each player starts with 8 zoo cards in hand and 25 money.
- **Action strength** — All action cards begin on side I (basic strength). Their power is determined by the slot they occupy (1 = weakest, 5 = strongest).

#### Player Count Adjustments
- **Solo mode** — Uses a dedicated solo opponent card system with automated actions.
- **2 players** — Some Association board slots may be blocked; follow the 2-player setup rules on the board.
- **3-4 players** — All Association board slots are available.

#### Key Reminders Before Starting
- **Hand limit** — There is no hand limit in Ark Nova. You may hold any number of cards.
- **Hidden information** — Your hand of zoo cards and your final scoring card are hidden from other players.
- **Display awareness** — The 6 face-up cards in the display are public information and available to all players.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Action Selection
- **Choose 1 action** — On your turn, select exactly 1 of your 5 action cards to play.
- **Strength = slot number** — The action's power equals the slot number it currently occupies (slot 1 = strength 1, slot 5 = strength 5).
- **X-token boost** — You may spend X-tokens to increase the action's effective strength by 1 per token spent.
- **Resolve action** — Carry out the action at its determined strength level.

#### Action Card Slide
- **Used card to slot 1** — After resolving, move the played action card to slot 1.
- **Shift remaining** — All action cards that were in lower-numbered slots than the played card slide up by one slot each.
- **Cycle management** — This sliding mechanism means each action gradually gains strength as other actions are played, creating a natural cycle.

#### End of Turn
- **Refill display** — If any face-up card slots in the display are empty, refill them from the deck by sliding existing cards toward the snap zone (position 6) and adding new cards at position 1.
- **Break check** — If the break token has reached the break threshold, resolve a break (income and upkeep phase) before the next player's turn.
- **Pass play** — The next player clockwise takes their turn.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### CARDS Action
- **Draw cards** — Draw a number of cards equal to the action's strength.
- **Source choice** — For each card drawn, choose to take from the face-up display OR draw blind from the top of the deck.
- **Display draws** — When taking from the display, you may choose any visible card regardless of position.
- **Reputation bonus** — At higher reputation levels, the upgraded CARDS II action lets you draw additional cards or filter more effectively.

#### BUILD Action
- **Build enclosures** — Spend money to place an enclosure on your zoo map. Enclosure sizes range from 1-space to 5-space.
- **Enclosure types** — Standard enclosures (any animal), large enclosures (5-space, for large animals), petting zoo (special small enclosure), reptile house (holds multiple small reptiles in one structure).
- **Cost scaling** — Larger enclosures cost more money. A 1-space enclosure is cheap; a 5-space enclosure is expensive.
- **Kiosks & pavilions** — Instead of an enclosure, you may build a kiosk or pavilion for income generation during breaks.
- **Map restrictions** — Enclosures must fit within your zoo map's grid. Some spaces are water or rocks and cannot be built on.
- **Cover bonuses** — When you build over certain map spaces, you gain the printed bonus (money, X-tokens, reputation, or other rewards).
- **Upgraded BUILD II** — The upgraded version reduces costs or grants additional building options.

#### ANIMALS Action
- **Play an animal** — Play 1 animal card from your hand onto your zoo by paying its money cost.
- **Enclosure requirement** — The animal must be placed into a matching empty enclosure of the correct size. A size-2 animal needs a 2-space (or larger) empty enclosure.
- **Gain appeal** — Immediately advance your appeal marker by the animal's appeal value.
- **Icon bonuses** — Many animals grant bonuses based on how many matching continent or animal-type icons you already have in your zoo (combo/synergy rewards).
- **Special abilities** — Some animals have one-time or ongoing abilities that trigger when played or provide persistent effects.
- **Upgraded ANIMALS II** — Allows you to play animals at reduced cost or gain additional bonuses.

#### ASSOCIATION Action
- **Send workers** — Place your association workers on available slots on the Association board. The number of actions you can take equals the action's strength.
- **Partner zoos** — Place a worker on a partner zoo to gain its unique bonus (cards, money, reputation, or special effects) and unlock that continent's tag for conservation scoring.
- **Universities** — Place a worker on a university to gain its bonus (often extra card draw, reputation, or special abilities).
- **Donate to conservation** — Place a worker to contribute to a conservation project, gaining conservation points if you meet the project's requirements (specific animal types or continent sets).
- **Reputation advancement** — Some association actions grant reputation, which unlocks action card upgrades and bonus rewards.
- **Upgraded ASSOCIATION II** — Grants additional worker placements or more powerful association options.

#### SPONSORS Action
- **Play a sponsor** — Play 1 sponsor card from your hand, paying any required cost.
- **Ongoing sponsors** — Some sponsors provide a permanent effect that applies for the rest of the game (e.g., discounts on animals, extra income during breaks).
- **One-time sponsors** — Some sponsors grant an immediate powerful bonus and are then discarded or placed face-down.
- **Placement** — Sponsor cards are placed near your zoo map as part of your tableau.
- **Strength requirement** — Some powerful sponsors require a minimum action strength to play.
- **Upgraded SPONSORS II** — Allows you to play sponsors at reduced cost or play additional sponsors.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Action Card Upgrading
- **Reputation thresholds** — When your reputation marker reaches certain thresholds on the reputation track, you may upgrade one of your action cards by flipping it to its side II.
- **Permanent improvement** — Upgraded actions are strictly better, offering enhanced versions of their base effects.
- **Strategic choice** — You choose which action card to upgrade when you hit the threshold, so prioritize the actions most critical to your strategy.
- **Priority upgrades** — Animals II and Association II are commonly upgraded first, as they provide the strongest improvements for most strategies.

#### X-Tokens
- **Wild resource** — X-tokens are versatile tokens that can be spent in multiple ways.
- **Boost action strength** — Spend 1 X-token to increase any action's effective strength by 1.
- **Substitute for money** — Use X-tokens as money when paying costs (1 X-token = 1 money, or better depending on context).
- **Earning X-tokens** — Gained from covering certain map spaces when building, from animal/sponsor abilities, and from some association actions.

#### Snapping
- **Snap condition** — When the card display shifts and a card would be discarded from position 6 (the rightmost slot), any player may snap it.
- **Snap cost** — Pay 1 money to take a card that is about to be discarded from the display.
- **Timing** — Snapping happens immediately when the card would leave the display; it is not part of the active player's action.
- **Competition** — If multiple players want to snap, priority goes to the player closest to the active player in clockwise order.

#### Break (Income & Upkeep)
- **Break trigger** — The break token advances whenever certain game events occur (specific card plays, association actions). When it reaches the break threshold, a break is triggered.
- **Income** — During a break, each player earns income based on their kiosks, pavilions, and other income-generating effects.
- **Upkeep** — Certain ongoing costs must be paid during breaks (though most zoo costs are one-time).
- **Card display reset** — The display may be partially or fully refreshed during a break.
- **Break token reset** — After resolving the break, the break token returns to its starting position.

#### Conservation Points
- **Conservation projects** — Gain conservation points by donating to conservation projects on the Association board that match your zoo's animal collection.
- **Releasing animals** — Some animals can be released from your zoo (removed from your enclosure) to gain conservation points. The enclosure becomes empty again for reuse.
- **Base vs. player projects** — The 8 base projects are always available. Players can also play conservation project cards from their hand to add new projects to the board.

#### Appeal Track & Icons
- **Appeal as VP** — Your position on the appeal track is your primary scoring metric. Animals and certain sponsors advance your appeal.
- **Continent icons** — Africa, Europe, Asia, Americas, Australia — animals display which continent(s) they originate from. Collecting sets of continents unlocks synergies.
- **Animal type icons** — Herbivore, predator, bird, reptile, primate, bear, and others — matching types trigger combo bonuses on many cards.
- **Tagging system** — Your zoo builds a collection of icons over time. Many cards reference "if you have X icons of type Y" for scaling rewards.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End Trigger
- **Track crossing** — The game end is triggered when any player's appeal marker and conservation marker cross each other on the intertwined scoring track (appeal counts up, conservation counts down from the opposite end).
- **Final round** — Once the crossing occurs, finish the current round so all players have had the same number of turns.

#### Final Scoring
- **Appeal position** — Note your appeal marker's position on the track.
- **Conservation position** — Note your conservation marker's position on the track.
- **Combined score** — Your final score equals the difference between your appeal position and your conservation position. The goal is for appeal to be as far ahead of conservation as possible (or more precisely, for both to be as high as possible so their meeting point on the track yields the highest score).
- **Final scoring card** — Resolve your kept final scoring card for bonus points (these typically award extra appeal or conservation based on meeting specific conditions like having certain animal types, a certain number of sponsors, etc.).

#### Determining the Winner
- **Highest score wins** — The player with the highest combined Appeal-minus-Conservation score wins.
- **Tiebreaker** — In case of a tie, the player with the most remaining money wins.
- **Second tiebreaker** — If still tied, the player with the most cards remaining in hand wins.

#### Typical Scoring Range
- **Competitive scores** — Experienced players typically aim for scores in the 20-40+ range, depending on player count and strategy.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
- **Build enclosures first** — Your first few turns should focus on the BUILD action to place enclosures on your map, as you cannot play animals without enclosures to house them.
- **Prioritize action card upgrades** — Reaching reputation thresholds to upgrade action cards (especially Animals and Association) is a top priority in the early game. Upgraded actions are dramatically more powerful.
- **Don't hoard cards** — While it's tempting to draw lots of cards early, focus on building your zoo infrastructure first. Cards without enclosures are dead weight.
- **Use the CARDS action at high strength** — Since the CARDS action benefits most from high strength (more draws), try to let it cycle up to slot 4 or 5 before using it.
- **Plan your final scoring card** — Your final scoring card shapes your entire strategy. If it rewards birds, prioritize bird animals. Build toward it from the start.
- **Secure early animals** — Playing 1-2 cheap animals early generates appeal and activates icon synergies for future cards.
- **Cover map bonuses** — When building enclosures, prioritize placements that cover valuable bonus spaces (X-tokens, reputation, money) on your zoo map.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
- **Appeal vs. conservation balance** — You need both tracks to be high for a good score. Neglecting either one leads to a poor result. Appeal comes from animals; conservation comes from projects and releasing animals.
- **Engine building through sponsors** — Sponsor cards that give discounts, extra income, or bonus draws compound over the game. Playing key sponsors early creates a strong engine for the mid and late game.
- **Timing the endgame** — The game ends when appeal and conservation cross. If you're ahead, push for the crossing. If you're behind, try to slow the game by avoiding conservation advancement.
- **Action card cycling** — Managing which slot each action card is in is a core puzzle. Sometimes you want to play a weaker action at low strength just to cycle a different action up to slot 5.
- **Enclosure sizing** — Building a 5-space enclosure is expensive but necessary for large high-appeal animals. Balance your enclosure sizes to accommodate both cheap small animals and expensive large ones.
- **University vs. partner zoo investments** — Universities provide card draw and flexibility, while partner zoos unlock continent bonuses for conservation. Your strategy dictates which to prioritize.
- **When to release animals** — Releasing an animal sacrifices its appeal contribution in your zoo but gains conservation points and frees the enclosure. Time releases carefully for maximum benefit.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
- **Ignoring conservation until too late** — New players often focus entirely on appeal (playing animals) and realize too late that their conservation score is too low to compete. Start working on conservation by mid-game at the latest.
- **Building enclosures you can't fill** — Every empty enclosure is wasted money and map space. Don't build speculatively unless you have animals in hand that need homes.
- **Not upgrading action cards early enough** — The leap from side I to side II is enormous. Players who delay upgrades fall behind in efficiency throughout the game.
- **Neglecting the action card cycle** — Playing actions in slot 1 or 2 repeatedly is inefficient. Plan your turns so that high-priority actions are at high strength when you need them.
- **Overvaluing expensive animals** — A 5-cost animal with 7 appeal looks great, but if you can't afford it without burning multiple turns earning money, cheaper animals played faster may generate more total value.
- **Ignoring the break track** — The break provides income. Players who build kiosks and pavilions early benefit from multiple break cycles. Ignoring income generation leads to money shortages.
- **Not scouting the display** — The 6 face-up cards are visible to everyone. Failing to snap a critical card or letting an opponent pick up a perfect synergy card is a common oversight.
- **Spreading icons too thin** — Focusing on too many continent and animal types dilutes your synergy bonuses. Concentrate on 2-3 icon types for maximum combo payoff.

metadata.notes: Ark Nova by Mathias Wigge. BGG top-3 ranked. Comprehensive rules covering all 5 action types, action card sliding mechanism, break system, and dual-track scoring.
